title: Meet the ESP32-S3 — what this chip actually is
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Understand what you're actually holding: a whole computer on one chip that runs a single program forever. Learn the board part by part, meet the pins your code will control, and learn the one voltage rule that keeps you from frying anything. No wiring or uploading yet — today buys you a mental model that makes every later day make sense.

sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: The small metal box soldered to the board is the ESP32-S3 module. Under the shield sit the processor, its memory, the flash chip that holds your program, and the radio for Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. The rest of the board exists to serve it — power, the USB port, buttons, and the pins that fan its signals out to the world. The shield keeps the radio clean and stops it interfering.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

coachInstructions: This is an orientation day — nothing to wire or upload — but it carries real ideas; don't let the learner skip them.
Land the core model first- a microcontroller is a whole computer on one chip that runs one program from power-on, and the uploaded sketch is that program.
Walk the board tour on request- metal can (the computer), USB-C port, BOOT/RESET buttons, onboard LED, the GPIO rows, and the pinout map.
Make sure they leave understanding what a GPIO pin is and the 3.3V rule- pins are 3.3V, powering a part from 5V is fine but feeding 5V into a pin can damage it.
Reinforce safe handling briefly- hold by the edges, mind static — as a reason, not a lecture.
steps: Find the metal can in the middle. That shield covers the whole computer — processor, memory, and the flash chip that will hold your program.
Find the USB-C port along one edge. Power and code both arrive here, and the board will talk back to your screen through it.
Find the two small buttons near the port. One is RESET, one is BOOT — together they control whether the board runs your program or listens for a new one.
Find the small onboard LED near the chip. It's wired to one pin and needs no external parts, which is why you'll blink it on Day 3 before touching a single wire.
Look along both long edges and find the two rows of numbered pins. Each one is a GPIO — a wire your program can switch on, or read a signal from.
Take the pinout map provided above and match a few of its printed numbers to the physical pins on the board. That map is how "GPIO 13" in code becomes a specific pin you can touch.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: A whole computer on one chip that runs one program forever — and speaks in 3.3 volts.
theoryModel.formula: one chip + one program + pins at 3.3V = everything you'll build

notes.title: Why the metal shield
notes.body: It keeps the Wi-Fi and Bluetooth radio inside clean and stops it leaking interference into the rest of the board.

notes.title: The power path
notes.body: USB gives 5V; the chip needs 3.3V. A regulator on the board steps it down — which is exactly why the pins are 3.3V devices.

challenge: Use the pinout to locate the power rails, the BOOT pin, and the onboard LED's pin, then reason about why 5V from a sensor can't go straight into a GPIO.
